query: PostgreSQL vs MySQL for time series data

text_blocks.snippet:
When it comes to managing time-series data, PostgreSQL is significantly better than MySQL. While neither vanilla database is a native time-series database (TSDB) out of the box, PostgreSQL provides an architecture, feature set, and extension ecosystem that makes it highly effective for time-stamped workloads


.
snippet_highlighted_words: PostgreSQL is significantly better than MySQL

text_blocks.snippet: Here is how they stack up across the key technical requirements for time-series:

list.snippet:
PostgreSQL: Has a massive superpower in TimescaleDB. TimescaleDB is an extension that transforms Postgres into a powerhouse native time-series database
. It introduces "hypertables" which automatically partition data by time, drastically improving write throughput and query speeds at scale while supporting features like native data compression and automated data retention (TTL).

MySQL: Lacks an equivalent, mature time-series extension. To handle large-scale time-series data in MySQL, you have to build complex, manual partitioning strategies, or stitch together proprietary tools



.

PostgreSQL: Excels at complex analytical queries. Its advanced query planner can effectively combine indexes (bitmap scans), handle parallel query plans, and seamlessly process advanced window functions
. This is crucial when you need to calculate moving averages, delta differences between time blocks, or downsample data over time.

MySQL: Is highly optimized for fast, simple read/write workloads (like lookups by ID). However, for heavy analytical grouping, complex JOIN
s, or intensive window functions spanning millions of time-series rows, MySQL often struggles and spills over into temporary tables.

PostgreSQL: Offers a wide variety of specialized index types. For time series, you can use BRIN (Block Range Indexes), which are incredibly small and performant for data that is naturally ordered by time
. This allows you to skip scanning irrelevant time periods without the massive storage overhead of traditional indexes.

MySQL: Is largely restricted to standard B-Tree indexes. As your time-series table grows into the tens or hundreds of millions of rows, B-Tree indexes become too massive to fit into memory, leading to severe write and read performance degradation
.
Choose MySQL only if: Your dataset is small-to-medium sized, your time-series requirements are very basic, and your team already has massive operational expertise in managing MySQL.

Choose PostgreSQL if:
You want a relational database that can truly scale with your time-series data. By utilizing vanilla Postgres (or instantly upgrading it with TimescaleDB), you get enterprise-grade time-series capabilities without needing to manage a completely separate NoSQL database silo.
